narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but other parts of your academic record raised concerns for the admissions model. This model looks at past students' profiles to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated your probability of failing at 57.5%, which tipped the scales toward rejection to minimize the school's risk of low bar passage rates. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and third-year high school decile ranking. Your strong LSAT was the most influential positive factor, pushing the model toward seeing you as likely to pass by showing sharp analytical skills that often correlate with bar success. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8—below the average 3.21—had a notable counteracting effect, signaling to the model potential struggles with sustained college-level performance compared to typical admitted students. Similarly, your fourth-decile ranking in Year 3 grades (average is fifth-decile) pulled the prediction toward failure, as it suggests your high school performance later on lagged behind peers who typically excel on the bar. Your middle family income bracket and full-time student status aligned with the norm—over 92% are full-time—so they didn't sway things much either way. Overall, while your LSAT gives you a real edge over many applicants, the model weighed your GPA and later high school decile more heavily as red flags when stacked against students who passed the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to widen that advantage, boosting your undergrad record through a post-bac program or relevant work experience, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your growth since high school. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these exact areas—your LSAT already proves you've got the raw ability. Keep pushing forward; law schools value persistence.
